upperAir: The KOAK sounding shows a very stable, shallow boundary layer with an extremely weak lapse rate of 2.26°C/km surface to 850mb — far below even moist adiabatic, indicating a strong inversion capping convection near the surface. Thermal development will be severely suppressed, with any BL growth limited to 400–470m AGL at best by afternoon per model data. Upper winds at 500mb are 30 kts from 270°, introducing significant wind shear above the inversion that could make upper-level conditions turbulent and unpredictable for higher launch sites.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the Bay Area SF region. Significant METAR anomalies: KHAF reporting sustained NNW 20-21 kts gusting 26-29 kts through the overnight/early morning period with 5-7SM haze — strong offshore-to-coastal gradient wind event in progress. KSFO showing 290° at 20 kts, peak gust 30 kts recorded at 0042Z. KRHV calm-ish at 330° 9-11 kts with scattered clouds at 10,000ft, temperatures 23-26°C overnight suggesting residual warmth inland. Buoy 46012 (Half Moon Bay) reporting 23.3 kts at 310° with 2.9m/9s swell; Bodega Bay 27.2 kts at 310°, SF Bar 23.3 kts at 320° — a strong NW gradient wind event is driving the overnight/morning coastal winds, SST 12.9-13°C (cold, supportive of persistent marine layer).

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — inland ridge, SW-facing primary, NW-facing upper launch
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's 1750ft launch uses valley thermals and upslope flow from the south bay. Ideal conditions require NW-to-W winds at 8-15 kts at launch with a functional thermal cycle driven by inland heating. The upper launch is primarily a thermal site with ridge-assist.
sites.todaySetup: The dominant story at Ed Levin 1750ft today is a chaotic, shifting wind regime in the morning that makes the launch window unreliable, followed by afternoon conditions that are too light and thermally weak to make worthwhile climbs. The sounding reveals a capped BL with an inversion near the surface — thermal tops will struggle to exceed 430m AGL (~1400ft MSL) even at peak heating around 1-2 PM, which is barely above the 1750ft launch elevation. There's a significant model disagreement between HRRR/GFS (showing NNE 9-12 kts morning, shifting NW by afternoon at 5-7 kts surface) and Tomorrow.io (showing NNE 6-10 kts with gusts to 20 kts by 1-3 PM at the 1750ft level). The Tomorrow.io gusts of 17-20 kts are a serious red flag for punchy, disorganized air given the unstable morning cloud cover (95-100% at 10-11 AM per HRRR) and rapid sky transitions. KHAF's overnight 20-21G29 kts NNW surface gradient is beginning to relax by morning but the coastal gradient pressure remains elevated. No viable thermal soaring window exists — the BL is too shallow to climb above launch, and afternoon winds are either too light or too gusty depending on which model verifies.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 8-12 kts at launch per HRRR, but 95-100% cloud cover by 10-11 AM completely shuts off solar heating. Tomorrow.io shows gusts only 4-7 kts surface at lower elevations but 80m winds at 15-21 kts — significant shear. Not launchable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Clouds clear rapidly around noon (HRRR drops to 2% cloud cover by 12 PM), winds shift to light NNW-NW at 4-6 kts surface. BL height peaks ~430m but that's below launch. Tomorrow.io shows gusts building to 17-20 kts by 1-2 PM — punchy and inconsistent.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds turn WNW at 6-8 kts surface, BL collapsing back after 5 PM with cloud returning (88-97% by 5-6 PM). Tomorrow.io gusts remain 13-17 kts through 5 PM. Too light for reliable soaring, and the evening cloud return signals end of any thermal window.

verdict.hazards: Inversion caps BL below launch altitude; chaotic morning wind shift with significant shear (21 kts at 80m vs 6-9 kts surface); Tomorrow.io gusts 17-20 kts in afternoon; cloud cover shutting off heating 10 AM–noon

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 600ft training launch at Ed Levin works on thermal-assisted valley breeze, ideally 8-14 kts from the W-SW. It's a beginner/intermediate site requiring clean, consistent upslope flow and a functioning thermal cycle to provide lift off the slope.
sites.todaySetup: The 600ft launch faces a similar problem to the upper launch but with different wind dynamics — morning NNE flow at 13-15 kts at 8 AM is cross to slightly on, but 80m winds are running 20-23 kts, creating dangerous shear between the surface and glider altitude within seconds of launch. By midday the winds drop to 3-7 kts NW-NNW which sounds more manageable, but Tomorrow.io shows gusts building to 16-18 kts from the NE by 2-4 PM, suggesting a punchy, uneven sea breeze front arrival. Cloud cover at 88% from 10-11 AM kills the thermal cycle at exactly the time when heating should be building. The BL height of 420-465m is marginally above the 600ft launch, but the capped inversion means thermals will be weak, turbulent, and short-lived. This is a training hill — the disorganized, gusty afternoon conditions are inappropriate even for supervised P2 students.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 13-15 kts surface with 80m winds at 20-23 kts — excessive shear, too strong. Cloud cover building to 88% by 10 AM kills heating. Not launchable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind drops to 4-7 kts NNW by noon — possibly the calmest window, but Tomorrow.io starts showing gusty NNE 11-16 kts by 1-2 PM. The brief calm period around 11-12 PM is the only potential window but thermals are absent due to morning cloud shutoff.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Tomorrow.io shows gusts of 16-18 kts from the NE/ENE through 4-5 PM. Evening cloud returns rapidly by 5-6 PM. No safe flying window for a training hill.

verdict.wind: 3–7 kts NW (midday), gusting to 16-18 kts afternoon
verdict.hazards: Morning shear (23 kts at 80m vs 13 kts surface); afternoon gusts 16-18 kts; cloud cover killing thermal cycle 10-11 AM; inversion capping BL near launch altitude

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.howItWorks: The 300ft bunny hill is a pure beginner training ground — works only on clean, gentle upslope breeze of 6-10 kts from the SW/W. Used exclusively for supervised P2 training flights and ground handling.
sites.todaySetup: The 300ft launch sees lighter surface winds than the upper launches, but the underlying atmospheric story is the same: the morning NW-to-NNW flow at 2-4 kts surface is far too light for any meaningful ridge lift, while 80m winds of 9-11 kts create shear that would be felt on a beginner glider immediately after launch. Tomorrow.io data for the lower elevation is nearly identical to the 600ft site, showing gusts building to 11-16 kts NNE in the early afternoon. For a 300ft training hill where students are doing their first ground runs and tandem-length flights, these conditions — with a gusty, shifting gradient, model disagreement, and a chaotic mid-morning cloud event — are unsuitable for supervised training. The KRHV overnight wind of 330° at 9-11 kts with temperatures still 23-26°C confirms the valley is still under the influence of a drainage/gradient wind rather than a clean thermal cycle.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Light and variable 2-4 kts surface, with 80m winds at 9-11 kts creating unsafe shear for beginners. Cloud cover to 78-82% by 10-11 AM. Not suitable.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Surface winds 5-8 kts NW-WNW — the most flyable-looking numbers of the day, but Tomorrow.io shows gusts 11-16 kts building from the NE. Not consistent enough for beginner training.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Winds WNW at 8-9 kts surface with gusts to 16-18 kts per Tomorrow.io. Evening cloud and BL collapse by 5 PM. Unsafe for beginner operations.

verdict.hazards: Shear between surface and 80m all day; gusty afternoon from Tomorrow.io; cloud cover disrupting thermal cycle; not appropriate for beginner training

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-to-NW facing ridge site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge-soaring site that runs on W-NW marine wind. Ideal conditions: 10-18 kts from 270-310°, clean flow off the ocean. Too light and you're sinking out; too strong (20+ kts) and the rotor behind the bluff becomes dangerous. Morning marine layer burn-off timing is critical.
sites.todaySetup: The coastal gradient wind event that hammered the coast overnight (KHAF 20-21G29 kts, buoys 23-27 kts from 310°) is the defining story here — but not in a good way for early morning. By the time the brief opens, winds at Mussel Rock are expected to be from the W-SW at 4-10 kts per HRRR, which sounds usable, but Tomorrow.io tells a very different story, showing 6-8 kts from NW-NNW with gusts to 11-14 kts early morning, then winds backing to SSW at 5-6 kts with only 4-5 kt gusts through the heart of the afternoon (1-4 PM) — too light for reliable ridge lift at a site that needs sustained 10+ kts onshore. The SST at Half Moon Bay is 12.9°C (cold), supporting a persistent marine layer, and KHAF's haze and 5SM visibility overnight suggests low stratus nearby even if KHAF itself is clear. The combination of decreasing afternoon winds (both models agree winds weaken 2-5 PM), patchy clouds (100% at 10 AM and again 90-100% by 5 PM per HRRR), and light afternoon winds from the SSW (not ideal W-NW) makes this a marginal day at best. The window around 1-3 PM where HRRR shows 7-10 kts from 261-263° could support soaring, but Tomorrow.io's lighter winds and south-backing direction suggest rotor risk in the bowl is elevated.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: HRRR shows 4-6 kts W-SW, Tomorrow.io shows 6-8 kts NW gusting to 11-14 kts. Cloud cover 42-100% per HRRR. Too variable and cloudy for a clean ridge session — model disagreement is concerning.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: HRRR shows 7-8 kts from due W (268°) — the most promising window. Tomorrow.io shows lighter SSW flow at 5-6 kts, gusts only 7 kts. If HRRR verifies, a short soaring window is possible; if Tomorrow.io verifies, sink-out conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: Both models agree winds weaken to 3-5 kts with backing to SW or S — too light for ridge lift. Cloud returns to 28-90% by 4-5 PM per HRRR, and precipitation chances tick up to 1-2%. Soaring window closes.

verdict.bestWindow: 11 AM – 1 PM (if HRRR W flow verifies)
verdict.wind: 7–10 kts W (midday HRRR) vs 5–6 kts SSW (Tomorrow.io)
verdict.hazards: Strong model disagreement on wind direction/speed; overnight gradient wind event leaving turbulent residual flow; SST 12.9°C cold marine layer; afternoon winds weakening and backing to SW; cloud cover 100% at 10 AM; precipitation risk 1-2% from 3 PM onward; rotor risk if south-backing winds verify

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal hybrid, S-to-W launch aspects
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works as a thermal site with ridge assist from the afternoon W-NW sea breeze. Ideal launch requires NW-to-W winds at 8-15 kts at the 2500ft summit with a developing thermal cycle in the valley below. The site is sensitive to the sea breeze front timing and can go strong quickly.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak shares the same upper-air environment as Ed Levin — an inversion-capped BL peaking at 430m AGL, which translates to roughly 2900ft MSL from the valley floor, giving perhaps 400ft of thermal climb above the 2500ft summit. That's insufficient for meaningful soaring. The morning NNE flow at 9-12 kts (HRRR) is off the back and potentially turbulent on launch, while Tomorrow.io shows only 2-4 kts from variable directions with gusts under 9 kts — far too light to soar a site that needs reliable W-NW flow. By afternoon, HRRR shows winds shifting to WNW at 6-7 kts with BL heights of 395-430m — marginal ridge assist at best. Tomorrow.io shows afternoon gusts of 10-13 kts from the NE-ENE at lower altitudes, suggesting a sea breeze front that hasn't organized cleanly. The 30% cloud at 1 PM and returning 88% cloud by 5 PM (HRRR) further suppress thermal development. Given the shallow, capped atmosphere and unreliable wind direction, Mission Peak offers no reliable soaring window today.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 9-12 kts at summit (HRRR) — off the back of the primary launch aspects. 95-100% cloud by 10-11 AM kills heating. Not launchable in useful conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Cloud clears rapidly by noon, winds lighten to 4-6 kts shifting toward NW-WNW. BL peaks at 405-430m AGL. Tomorrow.io shows only 3-5 kts surface with gusts 6-9 kts. Thermals too weak and capped to climb above launch.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2 PM–sunset)
hourlyWindows.summary: WNW 5-7 kts at summit, BL beginning to collapse. Tomorrow.io gusts 10-13 kts from ENE suggest disorganized sea breeze arrival. Cloud returning by 5 PM. Not a viable soaring window.

verdict.hazards: Inversion caps BL near summit altitude; morning NNE flow off the back; cloud cover 95-100% at 10-11 AM; disorganized afternoon wind direction; Tomorrow.io and HRRR disagree significantly on afternoon wind speed; BL insufficient for meaningful climb above launch

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday (March 22) looks more promising for Ed Levin upper launch. GFS shows NW 7-8 kts at surface by afternoon with BL heights rising to 595-680m AGL — significantly better than today, potentially reaching above the 1750ft launch. Morning cloud (93-100% at 8-10 AM per Tomorrow.io) will need to burn off. The best window looks like 12-4 PM with light NW flow and a modest thermal cycle. Tomorrow.io shows gusts only 9-11 kts — manageable. Assess morning cloud burn-off before committing.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday shows similar improvement with NW 5-8 kts afternoon, BL 410-595m AGL. Morning overcast (70-100% cloud) needs to clear. Afternoon window 11 AM–4 PM looks viable with light-moderate NW flow and modest thermals. Tomorrow.io gusts 7-9 kts — appropriate for this training site if the morning cloud clears on schedule.

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday afternoon looks usable for the training hill — NW 3-7 kts with gusts under 9 kts per Tomorrow.io, BL heights rising adequately. Morning overcast is the main concern. If cloud clears by 11 AM, a supervised training window of noon to 3 PM is plausible.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday shows a better setup for Mussel Rock with sustained W-WNW flow at 6-9 kts (HRRR/GFS) and Tomorrow.io showing 8-10 kts from 280-310° with gusts to 12-14 kts — well within the site's ideal range. Morning cloud (100% at 8 AM per Tomorrow.io) should clear by mid-morning. The afternoon window of 10 AM–4 PM looks the best of the week for this site. P3+ recommended.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday shows light winds at Mission Peak all day (GFS surface 2-4 kts morning, building to 4-5 kts WNW by afternoon) with BL heights rising to 580-640m — better but still marginal. The 80m winds show 7-10 kts WNW by afternoon which could support weak ridge assist. Not a strong thermal day but better than Saturday. Monitor morning cloud burn-off.

day2.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday March 23 shows improved conditions with BL heights reaching 690-760m AGL — well above the 1750ft launch. Light NW 7-9 kts in the afternoon (GFS) with minimal cloud cover. A legitimate soaring day is possible, though early morning light SE winds and 1% precipitation chance need watching.

day2.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday looks good for the training hill — NW 5-8 kts afternoon, BL well above 600ft, minimal cloud. A quality training day if the light morning precip risk (1%) doesn't materialize.

day2.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day2.analysis: Monday afternoon should work well for the beginner hill — light NW breeze, deep BL, clear skies forecast. Best day of the 4-day window for this site.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Monday March 23 shows W-WNW 6-8 kts (GFS) with mostly clear skies. Looks like a solid ridge soaring day, though winds may be lighter than ideal. Tomorrow.io not available at this range but the setup looks consistent. Worth planning for.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Monday shows very light surface winds at Mission Peak (3-5 kts) but 80m winds building to 9-11 kts WNW by afternoon — possibly enough for ridge assist with thermals. BL reaching 500-725m is the best of the 4-day outlook. Worth a look for P3 pilots.

day3.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday March 24 (Day 3 of GFS forecast, lower confidence): Light NW 7-9 kts afternoon, BL similar to Monday at 680-760m. Looks like a continuation of improving conditions. Check updated models as the date approaches.

day3.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday shows similar NW flow with adequate BL. Afternoon cloud (49-64%) may limit thermal development. Watchlist item — monitor cloud forecast.

day3.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
day3.analysis: Tuesday afternoon looks okay for training — NW 6-8 kts with clearing possible after morning. The 49-64% afternoon cloud is a concern for thermal quality.

day3.name: Mussel Rock
day3.analysis: Tuesday shows W-NW 6-8 kts continuing, light cloud. Looks like another ridge soaring opportunity similar to Monday. Confidence moderate at Day 3 range.

day3.name: Mission Peak
day3.analysis: Tuesday at Day 3 GFS range shows 80m winds reaching 9-11 kts WNW by afternoon with BL 500-725m — similar to Monday. Afternoon cloud 38-65% may limit thermals. Light surface winds remain the concern.

watchlist: Strong coastal gradient wind event overnight (KHAF 20-21G29 kts, buoys 23-27 kts NW) — residual turbulence and rotor risk at Mussel Rock through mid-morning
Significant model disagreement between HRRR/GFS and Tomorrow.io at all sites today — treat afternoon wind forecasts with caution
Inversion and capped BL (430m AGL max today) keeps thermal tops below Ed Levin upper launch elevation — fundamental limitation for today
Cold SST 12.9-13°C at Half Moon Bay and SF Bar buoys — supports persistent marine influence and late burn-off at coastal sites
Morning cloud event (95-100% cloud cover at 10-11 AM at Ed Levin per HRRR) will kill the thermal cycle at critical solar heating hours
Tomorrow.io shows gusts 16-20 kts in the afternoon at Ed Levin sites from NNE — punchy sea breeze front arrival not well-organized
tldr: Today is essentially a no-fly day across the board — a strong coastal gradient wind event overnight is relaxing but leaving disorganized, model-disagreed conditions, and an inversion caps the BL below the Ed Levin upper launch altitude all day. Mussel Rock has the only marginal window (11 AM–1 PM if HRRR's W flow verifies at 7-10 kts) but Tomorrow.io paints it lighter and south-backed, so P3+ locals only and check conditions on arrival. Sunday looks meaningfully better across all sites, especially Mussel Rock — worth saving the energy for that.
